coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots: Review of recent applications and perspectives in bio-sensing and biomarker detection
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.

coredata.dc:creator: Terzapulo, Xeniya
Kassenova, Aiym
Loskutova, Alissa
Bukasov, Rostislav
coredata.dc:description: Carbon Dots (CDs) are carbon-based nanoparticles with relatively strong and tunable fluorescence emission properties and relatively good photo stability, which are increasingly used in sensing and biosensing as reported in hundreds of research publications. There are 4 types of CD-based sensors or detection (CD-only, CD-based Enzymatic, CD-based Antigen-Antibody modified, CD-based Nucleic acid biosensors) that will be described and compared in this review. Such analytical parameters as Limit of Detection (LOD), linear range, Relative Standard Deviation (RSD) are compared and discussed in this review. Median and/or average LOD, RSD, Linear Range are calculated and presented in multiple tables, while LODs are tabulated from 135 references. Among these biosensors, CD-based Nucleic Acid biosensors and CD-based Antibody-Antigen biosensors show better sensitivity (lower LOD) when compared with CD-only and CD-based Enzymatic biosensors. Four main groups of detection techniques applicable, such as fluorescence, electrochemical, colorimetry, and electrochemiluminescence methods are considered and compared for CD-based Antibody-Antigen biosensors descriptions. Perspectives of machine learning methods and smart phones as read out devices for CD-based bio-sensors are outlined in the conclusion of the review. We also briefly described and illustrated with graphic examples/figures some basic phenomena related to CD-based sensing, including CD doping, up-conversion, modification and immobilization of CDs, quenching and FRET.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots: Review of recent applications and perspectives in bio-sensing and biomarker detection

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon Dots (CDs) are carbon-based nanoparticles with relatively strong and tunable fluorescence emission properties and relatively good photo stability, which are increasingly used in sensing and biosensing as reported in hundreds of research publications. There are 4 types of CD-based sensors or detection (CD-only, CD-based Enzymatic, CD-based Antigen-Antibody modified, CD-based Nucleic acid biosensors) that will be described and compared in this review. Such analytical parameters as Limit of Detection (LOD), linear range, Relative Standard Deviation (RSD) are compared and discussed in this review. Median and/or average LOD, RSD, Linear Range are calculated and presented in multiple tables, while LODs are tabulated from 135 references. Among these biosensors, CD-based Nucleic Acid biosensors and CD-based Antibody-Antigen biosensors show better sensitivity (lower LOD) when compared with CD-only and CD-based Enzymatic biosensors. Four main groups of detection techniques applicable, such as fluorescence, electrochemical, colorimetry, and electrochemiluminescence methods are considered and compared for CD-based Antibody-Antigen biosensors descriptions. Perspectives of machine learning methods and smart phones as read out devices for CD-based bio-sensors are outlined in the conclusion of the review. We also briefly described and illustrated with graphic examples/figures some basic phenomena related to CD-based sensing, including CD doping, up-conversion, modification and immobilization of CDs, quenching and FRET.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, a large number of materials are based on carbon. Examples of carbon-based structures are fullerene, graphene, carbon nanotubes, nanodiamonds, their derivatives, as well as other carbon allotropes [1]. These materials are actively used due to their size, shape, as well as properties such as thermal and electrical conductivity [2]. In addition to the above-mentioned materials, there is another type of carbon-based structure, which is now actively gaining popularity in materials science. Carbon dots (CDs) are a very promising material that is used in research due to their small size (less than 10 nm), chemical stability, relatively low toxicity, as well as a variety of functional groups [3]. Carbon dots were first investigated in 2004, when analyzing fluorescent single-wall fragments of carbon nanotubes (SWNT) [4]. Walter A. Scrivens' research group revealed the absence of metals and presence of carboxyl groups in the structure of purified long and cut SWNT by characterizing this material using UV light [4]. In their research, elemental analysis showed that the structure consists of 53.93 % carbon, 2.56 % hydrogen, 1.20 % nitrogen, and 40.33 % oxygen [4].

Since the structure of carbon dots is quite diverse, it is difficult to classify them. Despite this, there are 4 main categories of CDs: carbon quantum dots (CQDs), graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon nanodots (CNDs), and carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) [5]. CQDs occur in nature as crystalline based on mixture of sp2 and few sp3 hybridization states, they are nanospheres and also have many chemical groups in their structure [6]. GQDs are single- or multi-layered graphene, with a diameter of less than 20 nm and a height of about 2.5 nm [6]. Unlike SQDs, only sp2 domains occur in GQDs [3]. CNDs are amorphous, and unlike the aforementioned carbon dots species, carbon nanodots lack the quantum entrapment effect. In addition, CNDs differ from SQDs and GQDs in that their structure also contains a mixture of sp2 and sp3, with more sp3 forms [6]. CPDs are based on carbon, which is surrounded by polymer structures and various functional groups. Just like carbon nanodots, carbonized polymer dots are amorphous, and sp3 is predominantly found in them [6].

There are many mechanisms for the synthesis of carbon dots, depending on the desired functions and the application of the resulting structures, which can be divided into 2 main groups: “top-down” and “bottom-up” [7]. The “top-down“strategy is used in the synthesis of 2D nanoparticles, which include graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [7]. The top-down approach includes methods such as arc discharge, chemical and electrochemical oxidation, laser ablation, and other methods and as sources carbon fiber uses large solids or powder [8]. Bottom-up strategy is to apply methods such as hydrothermal/solvothermal, combustion, microwave-assisted, and other methods for the synthesis of 3D nanoparticles, which include CQDs and CPDs [7,8]. In this type of CDs synthesis, polymers as a carbon source or small organic molecules [8]. Fig. 1 illustrates the general working process of each of these methods.

Laser ablation is the method introduced in 1962 by Breech and Cross [9]. This method is based on the surface layer modification using intensive laser light energy [10]. The working mechanism of electrochemical oxidation (EC oxidation) is based on redox reactions, where oxidation occurs on the anode, whereas reduction occurs on the cathode [11]. This method is actively used nowadays due to its cost-effectiveness, versatility, and efficiency [11]. Arc discharge is one of the most efficient and popular methods used in carbon nanomaterial production due to the high yield and relatively low metallic impurities in products [12]. This method was firstly introduced in carbon nanotubes formation process in Sumio Iijima's research published in 1991 [13]. In arc discharge, the electrical discharge of the gas is initiated by breakdown pulse to generate the plasma [14]. Solvothermal method is frequently used to accelerate the reactions between solids or increase the solubility of some solids by using the solvent under high pressure and temperature [15]. Working mechanism of hydrothermal reactions is a solution-reaction approach based on the application of different temperatures and pressures which vary due to the product of desire and reaction components used [16]. Microwave-assisted method for chemical synthesis was presented for the first time in 1986 in the work of Gedye's and Giguere's research groups [17,18]. Microwaves as an energy source are used in research to activate chemical reactions despite microwave radiation [19]. This method is actively used in such processes as melting, drying, and sintering [19]. Combustion can be described as a self-propagating high-temperature synthesis (SHS) method by performing exothermic reactions [20]. One of the advantages of combustion lies in the fact that the size and morphology of desired products can be monitored, so the various materials can be produced using this method [20].

Due to the properties of CDs, as well as their relatively simple synthesis, carbon dos is actively used in many fields. One of the main applications of VTs is their use in biotechnology, as well as in drug delivery systems, bioimaging, and biosensing [6]. CDs are also actively used in cancer diagnosis and treatment, due to their advantages like large surface area, small size, and simple functionalization [8]. Thanks to the use of CDs, which are activated by red or other radiation, it becomes possible to choose effective methods for the treatment of tumors, since these carbon dots can penetrate deep damaged tissues [8]. Carbon dots are also actively used in chemical sensing and fluorescence imaging [21].

MS/MS), spectroscopy (UV–Vis, NMR, IR, RS) and other methods are used to characterize carbon dots [5]. These techniques allow you to collect the largest amount of information about carbon dots, such as size, shape, elemental composition, and other similar properties [5].

Surface enhanced fluorescence (SEF) and Metal Enhanced fluorescence (MEF) are also frequently used in biosensors working principle of which depends in the using of the carbon or quantum dots. There are biosensors which were developed to detect glucose, SARS-CoV-2 spike protein, alpha-fetoprotein (AFP), vascular endothelial growth factor-165 (VEGF165), and many other analytes by combining SEF or MEF with QDs or CDs [29–32].

For instance carbon dots were used as signal enhancers for the detection of E. coli cells on Au film with metal enhanced fluorescence (MEF) with calculated enhancement factor (EF) close to 50, S/N about 200, and contrast as high as 66 [33]. Even better MEF results were obtained when they applied CD dotes synthesized from date pits on Al foil which, among other substrates, had shown the highest contrast (217), and S/N (728) [34]. In another study relatively toxic and relatively non-toxic quantum dots (CdTe and CdSeS/ZnS) were tested and compared on several substrates (Au, Al and Ag films) for detection of bacterial cells and the conclusion was that low toxicity of substrates and low toxicity of quantum dots is strongly correlated with high contrast and high enhancement factor values for MEF, thus low toxicity composite QDs or organic CDs combined with cost-effective substrate such as Al foil appear to be a promising combination for the detection of single cells by SEF/MEF [35].

There are several reviews about carbon dot applications in biosensing [36–39]. However, we can hardly find systematic quantitative analysis of analytical performance parameters (LOD, RSD, range, etc.) in those reviews, which have been reported in recent research literature. In this review, we classified the detection of biomarkers with CD-based methods in 4 categories of analytical techniques, tabulated their applications in 4 tables, the calculated average and median values for the reported LODs as well as averages for some other analytical Figures of Merit (FoM) like RSD for each of those 4 categories. We compared the analytical performance of those methods and described some perspectives of improvements in CD-based bioanalytical applications, including for instance an application of machine learning in data analysis.

Hereafter we described some major methods to improve carbon dot performance in sensing including CD doping, modification of CDs and Immobilization of CDs on solid substrates or on nanoparticle surfaces. We also describe some major principles of CDs applications with detected fluorescence such as up-conversion and quenching of CD-fluorescence, including FRET.

Carbon dots are widely applied in cell imagining and biosensing due to their low toxicity, excellent optical properties, and good biocompatibility [40]. However, the application of bare CDs in biosensing can be limited due to such factors as weak ECL emission, wide ECL excitation potentials, poor electric conductivity, and small size to form a network for electron transfer, low catalytic activity, and low quantum yield [40–43]. Thus, heteroatom doping strategy is applied to modulate the electronic and luminescent properties of CDs and increase their catalytic activity. Such variations as nitrogen, phosphorus, selenium, boron, and metal doped CDs exist and are widely applied.

Application of CDs in the ECL field is limited by their weak ECL emissions and wide excitation potentials. Heteroatom doping causes the change in the electronic structure of CDs which improves their ECL intensity, however for single-doped CDs and some codoped CDs high concentration of coreactant is required as long as wide excitation potential to show strong ECL efficiency [48,49]. To overcome these limitations, Li and Yuan's group have introduced B,N co-doped CDs which were synthesized hydrothermally from citric acid, ethylenediamine and boric acid as the sources of carbon, nitrogen, and boron respectively ( Fig. 2b). N-doping narrowed the bandgap between HOMO and LUMO, which resulted in the increased ECL efficiency and positive shift of cathodic excitation potential, while B-doping exhibited the conversion into boron radicals through electrically excited and accelerating coreactants which lead to the generation of extra active radicals and following enhancement of ECL efficiency. Thus, B,N co-doped CDs have exhibited a much better performance than CDs without doping and single-doped CDs [40].

Kaimal et al. have synthesized B-GQDs from glucose and boric acid hydrothermally ( Fig. 2c) to utilize these nanostructures as the conducting electrode in their electrochemical biosensor for guanine and adenine detection. Similar sizes of carbon and boron allow for doping of B by swapping a C atom in the lattice structure of graphene quantum dots. Due to the electron-deficiency of B, there is a positive charge on the boron atoms integrated with carbon atoms, hence the p-type conducting behavior of graphene is improved, thus increasing the electron transfer ability of GQDs and making them a suitable option for a strongly efficient electron transfer which is useful for electrochemical applications. In the composites of NPs and heteroatom-doped QDs, while the intrinsic properties of each component retained, there are multiple synergistic advantages provided through cooperative contact which results in the increase of surface area, higher electrocatalytic activity, stronger biocompatibility and better electron transfer [41]. After doping with N atoms CDs become n-type, and when P acts as a n-type donor, substitutional defect is caused in sp3-bonded diamond films, which allows to alter the electronic properties of CDs and increase the amount of active sites [50,51]. Previously reported N,P c-doped CDs required high synthesis temperature up to 900 °C [52]. However, hydrothermal approach developed by Shi et al. allows for high output, satisfactory water solubility and outstanding fluorescence characteristics which allowed to detect Fe3+ with LOD 1.8 nM. Apart from that, prepared CDs were biocompatible and not cytotoxic which allows there application for the detection of Fe3+ in human serum and intracellular imaging of Fe3+.

Figure 2d demonstrates the TEM image of N, P co-doped CDs which are shown to be well monodispersed. They were prepared hydrothermally from glucose, ammonia and phosphoric acid. In the selected region size of N,P-CDs varies from 2.2 to 3.5 nm with 63 % having the size of 3.0 nm. Inset HRTEM image of a single N,P-CD shows that a particle has highly crystalline structure with lattice parameter being 0.20 nm that resembles sp2 graphitic carbon's diffraction plane [42]. Bandi et al. have utilized Fe-doped CDs (FeCDs) for the design of biosensor for the detection of hydrogen peroxide and glucose. FeCDs were synthesized using domestic microwave from citric acid and FeCl2*4H2O mixed in the nole ratio of 1:0.1. Spectra shown on the Fig. 2e demonstrate a 110 nm Stokes shift between excitation (370 nm) and emission (480 nm) wavelengths which is attributed to the Fe, i.e., dopant presence in the nanostructures [43]. Doping metals into CDs allows for tuning of intrinsic electronic characters of CDs and makes more active sites available for the reaction, while metal‑carbon synergism in the lattice enhances the enzyme-mimic activity of metal-doped CDs [53,54].

Figure 2f shows the schematic image of F, N co-doped CDs designed by Yuan and Chai's group. FNCDs were prepared solvothermally from glucose and triethylamine trihydrofluoride and shown themselves as an efficient ECL emitter. Multiatom doping, although enhances the ECL efficiency of CDs, is sometimes insufficient due to free rotation of CDs which increases the non-radiative transition and put significant challenge on the synthesis of multiatom co-doped CDs. This problem was solved by the authors via the synthesis of strongly electronegative FNCDs where hydrogen bonds between fluorine and hydrogen atoms hindered the free rotation of FNCDs and thus decreased non-radiative transition which lead to strong ECL response and excellent ECL stability [45].

In another publication, Chai and Yuan's group have synthesized selenium and nitrogen co-doped CDs (SeN-CDs) schematically shown on Fig. 2g via the hydrothermal approach from citric acid and selenocystine to design the ECL biosensor for the detection of microRNA. Introducing selenium as a dopant for CDs creates more redox active sites which is connected with its outstanding redox activity. Beyond that, selenium accelerates the correction via the acceleration of the formation olf abundant sulfate radicals produced form the coreactant S2O8 2− which significantly increases the ECL intensity. Resulting particles were of spherical shape and had the size of around 2 nm [47].

Both immobilization of CDs on solid surface (e.g., on electrode surface of chemiluminescence based sensors) and modification of CDs with sensing molecules such as antibodies or aptamers, which have high affinity for analyte, are important methods in the development of CD-based biosensors. For example, Mohammadi et al. have immobilized CDs in the chitosan-based hydrogel where chitosan was bound to the aldehyde groups of CDs via an imine bond [55]. Chowdhury and coworkers have designed a sandwich-type immunosensor where antibody-modified CDs were used as fluorescent labels [56]. On the other hand, modification of CDs with aptamers for biosensing purposes is also possible, and compared to antibodies, which are immunogenic and prone to denaturation, aptamers possess such characteristics as the possibility to use them in different buffers due to their stability at room temperature, and apart from that, they can be applied in bioimaging [57]. The examples of both Immobilization and Modification of CDs are visually represented on Fig. 3 .

The study by Yang et al. represented on Fig. 3a, shows the example of the development of an immobilization-based highly sensitive sensor towards 4-nitrophenol. The sensor is based on composite material synthesized from amine‑carbon quantum dots (amine-CQDs) and zirconium-based metal-organic framework (UiO-66). 4-Nitrophenol is a toxic environmental pollutant which is crucial to monitor. Citric acid and ethylenediamine, as carbon and nitrogen sources respectively, were used as precursors for CQDs. The dots exhibited blue fluorescence at 440 nm after excitation at 365 nm, remaining unchanged in fluorescence intensity for at least 10 days, implying high stability. UiO-66 is synthesized through a solvothermal reaction using ZrCl₄ and terephthalic acid. After the formation of the metal-organic framework, amine-CQDs are immobilized onto its structure. In the newly synthesized composite, MOF UiO-66 acts as adsorbent selectively interacting with the analyte increasing the selectivity of the method, whereas amine-CQDs act as a source of fluorescence signal which is quenched by interaction of adsorbent with 4-nitrophenol. The resulting composite exhibits high selectivity, low LOD of 3.5 nM and dynamic range of 0.01–20.0 mcM [58].

The study by Sun et al. presented in Fig. 3b focuses on application of chemiluminescent sensor. The sensor consists of graphene oxide (GO)@magnetic β-cyclodextrin polymers with surface immobilized adenosine aptamers. CQDs modified with single-stranded DNA (ss-DNA) are attached to the aptamer functionalized surface and are not present in the solution. When the adenosine is added, ss-DNA modified CQDs are detached from aptamers due to specific bindings between adenosine and its aptamer. The carbon dots then catalyze the CL reaction. The intensity of emitted light in the reaction is proportional to adenosine concentration. Fig. X (b) shows the graph which compares the response of chemiluminescent sensor's response from standard analyte solution and one with added interfering species. As it is demonstrated, the application of aptamers improves the selectivity of the method dramatically raising the multiples value by almost order of a magnitude in contrast to conventional methods. Hence, the article presents an efficient, cost-effective, and ultrasensitive biosensor for adenosine detection with LOD of 0.21 pM, with potential applications in medical diagnostics, particularly for cancer monitoring. This work illustrates how application of aptamers in sensing can significantly improve the selectivity of the method thanks to the aptamer-analyte specific binding [59].

Application scheme of near infrared CQDs modified with aptamers is shown on Fig. 3c. The carbon dots were synthesized hydrothermally from glutathione and formamide. Then the specific to cortisol aptamer was covalently bonded to quantum dots to form complex that experience fluorescence. Finally, NIR-CQDs-Apt complex is immobilized on the surface of graphene oxide surface by π- π stacking which quenches the emission from dots via possibly FRET or inner-effect filter mechanism. When cortisol is introduced, it binds specifically to aptamer leading to detachment of dots from GO surface restoring the intensity of fluorescence. The usage of aptamer specific to the analyte allows to significantly enhance the specificity of the sensing in comparison to existing sensors. Even if analyte's analogs are caught by the sensor, it has almost no effect on fluorescence intensity. The suggested detection method was also highly sensitive with detection limit of 0.13 nM. Altogether, the sensor was demonstrated to have great biocompatibility and high-resolution cellular imaging ability in living cells making it a promising tool for medicine area [57].

The Wang's group have developed an ECL immunosensor for carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) where primary antibody was immobilized on the nanocomposite of polydopamine (PDA) and AgNPs, while CQDs and AuNPs modified complex of graphene oxide functionalized with poly (ethylenimine) (PEI-GO) (Fig. 3d) served as a platform to immobilize secondary antibody. Synergistic effects of AgNPs, PDA, AuNPs and PEI-GO significantly enhanced the ECL signals of CQDs by serving as a good conductive material by increasing the rate of electron transfer and thus improving the electrochemical sensing ability of the biosensor [60].In the immunosensor designed by Chowdhury and coworkers, OCDs were conjugated with IgG through a carbodiimide coupling reaction which required the modification of OCDs with carboxyl groups. The basis of the carbodiimide chemistry lies in the activation of –COOH groups by EDC and NHS which allows them to react directly with –NH2 groups of IgG to form an amide bond (–CONH–) which is schematically shown on the Fig. 3e. As can be seen from the graph on the Fig. 3f, IgG-OCDs have demonstrated twice as high fluorescence intensity compared to non-conjugated bare OCDs. Quantum yields calculated for OCDs and IgG-OCDs were almost the same, being equal to 17.8 % and 17.25 % respectively, while average lifetime for the IgG-OCDs was 12.5 % shorter (2 ns vs. 1.75 ns respectively). Authors have concluded that such an increase in fluorescence intensity was connected with the increase in kr, the radiative rate constant, whose values were 0.08 ns-1 and 0.098 ns-1 respectively for OCDs and IgG-OCDs [56].

Sridara et al. have designed a sensing platform based on nanocomposites of CDs immobilized on CuO nanoparticles (Fig. 3g) for the detection of glucose with such outstanding properties as large active surface area and strong electrocatalytic activity. Sensitivity of the biosensor was improved in comparison with previously reported materials which can be attributed to the synergistic effects of CuO-C-dots nanocomposite that resulted in excellent catalytic activity for the oxidation of glucose by CuO nanoparticles. Other advantageous characteristics of the designed nanocomposite electrode include increased surface area and prevention of agglomeration of copper oxide NPs from CDs, and apart from that, the proposed sensor does not require a difficult fabrication process [61].

Up-conversion is a photophysical process where the emission wavelength of a nanoparticle happens to have more energy than the excitation one. In contrast to the more common in CQDs sub-conversion (or down-conversion), up-one results in blue shift. First studied in 2004, some CQDs were reported to natively exhibit up-conversion fluorescence; however, subsequent studies revealed that this observed up-conversion was often due to experimental artifacts, such as the leakage of second-order diffraction light from the monochromators in spectrophotometers, and application of long pass filters immediately neglected suspected up-conversion fluorescence [62]. Despite this, genuine up-conversion behavior was subsequently demonstrated in CQDs obtained both with direct synthesis or with modifications, including heterogeneous atoms doping or surface engineering.

Though photoluminescence up-conversion is typically a combination of several basic principles depending on specific parameters of dots, three generally accepted mechanisms explaining the increase in energy of fluorescence during the up-conversion are photon avalanche, excited state absorption, and energy transfer up-conversion [63].

In photon avalanche a nonlinear process occurs where a small increase in excitation intensity triggers a rapid, cascading increase in emission intensity which is commonly met in rare-earth doped systems rather than in CQDs. In excited state absorption, an electron in an already excited state absorbs a second photon, moving to an even higher energy level before emitting a photon of higher energy. Finally, during energy transfer up-conversion, energy is transferred from one or more excited particles to another, effectively “pooling” energy so that one particle reaches a higher excited state with blue-shifted photon emission [64].

In general, down-conversion possesses superior analytical performance because it occurs more often than up-one, as down-conversion typically follows a straightforward energy relaxation pathway. However, up-conversion quantum dots experience less interference from biological background luminescence since they are excited at longer wavelengths which is preferred for deep-tissue bioimaging or anti-Stokes emission-based sensing making them more suitable for bioimaging compared to down-ones. [65] What is more, they have longer fluorescence lifetimes.

The LOD and dynamic range can be better in any of the mechanisms. For instance in the case in the case of Fe3+ sensing in water and human serum by erbium-doped graphene quantum dots, 2.8 nM LOD was obtained for down-conversion which is 10 times smaller than that of up-conversion, though the linear range for the latter was slightly wider [66]. In other instances slight decline in LOD was achieved while using up-conversion instead of sub-one as in case of aspartate detection by disruption of graphene quantum dots/europium ions aggregates with 0.18 μM and 0.33 μM [67].

Solute fluorescence quenching is a research tool mostly applied in studies of proteins, membranes, and other macromolecules and it was introduced in the end of the 1960s and in early 1970s [68]. The fluorescence quenching is relatively easy to perform, it needs a relatively small amount of sample, proceeds in a nondestructive manner, and it is applicable to systems with extrinsic or intrinsic fluorescence dye [68]. Many applications of CDs in the detection of analytes, bioimaging and drug delivery are accomplished due to the decrease of fluorescence using quenching or increase in fluorescence by preventing quenching [69]. This quenching method is performed by different mechanisms such as static and dynamic quenching, photoinduced electron transfer (PET) and inner filter effect (IFE), and energy transfer, that is divided to Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), Dexter energy transfer (DET) and Surface energy transfer (SET) [69].

In Fig. 4 (a), red-emitting carbon dots (RCDs) were mixed with the solution of cobalt oxyhydroxide (CoOOH) nanoflakes and fabricated the quenching systme of RCDs/CoOOH by the transfer of energy by those nanoflakes [70]. The fluorescence for the detection was recovered due to the decomposition of nanoflakes into cobalt (II) ions making the surface with RCDs to be break down [70]. This is due to the hydrolysis reaction of ascorbic acid-2-phosphate trisodium salt (AAP) catalyzed by acid phosphatase (ACP), that formed the the ascorbic acid (AA) [70]. The suggested method demonstrates “off-on” principle for the sensitive detection of the ACP biomarker [70].

Chen et al. applied hydrothermal carbonization at 200°C for 5h in order to improve the properties of the CDs [71]. Following that, they reacted with mercaptopropylamine (MPA) to form thiol-functionalized CDs (S-CDs). As shown in Fig. 4 (b), there was only one peak represented approximately at 280 nm. When glucose was introduced, the glucose oxidase (GOx) catalyzes the conversion of the hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂) and then (H₂O₂) ameliorates the oxidation of the S-CDs, which later aggregate to larger aggresomes [71]. The formation of the (H₂O₂), thus, leads to the quenching of the fluorescence [71]. There is also “off” principle for the operation as the production of (H₂O₂) is dependent on the concentration of glucose [71]. Thus, the system detects the glucose according to the fluorescence changes [71]. Wu et al. suggest that some metals ions such as Ag+ and Cu2+ play an oxidizing role for o-phenylenediamine (OPD) and coordination between analyte and these metal ions may interfere with the redox reaction [72]. Hence, as shown in Fig. 4 (c), Wu et al. proposed a ratiometric fluorescence detection for adenosine triphosphate (ATP) where oxidation of OPD is triggered by cupper ions and inhibited by ATP [72]. The yellow emission occurred due to the oxidization of OPD by copper (II) ions forming oxOPD and quenching the CDs' blue fluorescence [72]. Nevertheless, the redox reaction for OPD is inhibited by the coordination of Cu2+ and ATP, quenching the fluorescence of oxOPD and reforming the CDs' fluorescence, which explain the contrast from yellow color to blue [72]. Thus, the method suggests the enhanced selectivity for the ATP detection [72].

In Fig. 4 (d), Shao et al. developed a dual-colored and ratiometric fluorescent sensor for the detection of ALP [73]. It is referred that finding two compatible CDs emitting various colors at the same excitation light and without interacting FRET is challenging [73]. The operation of the sensor was based on the FRET interactions of the between blue light emitted CDs-MnO2 (B-CDs-MnO₂) nanohybrid and yellow light emitted CDs-MnO2 (Y-CDs-MnO₂) nanohybrid [73]. The reaction of the B-CDs and Y-CDs with MnO₂ through the π-π stacking leads to the quenching of the fluorescence [73]. When ascorbic acid-2-phosphate (AAP) is dephosphorylated by ascorbic acid (AA), as shown in Fig. 4 (d), the MnO₂ are reduced to Mn2+ ions, recovering the fluorescence of the CDs [73].

Likely the most commonly used mechanism of quenching applicable for sensing with CDs is The Förster (or Fluorescence) Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET). FRET transfers energy from a fluorophore to a chromophore under the dipole-dipole interaction, without emitting a photon [74]. This process depends on the orientation of the dipoles, the spectral overlap, and the distance between the donor and the acceptor. In this process, the excited fluorophore (donor) is located at a distance of 1 to 10 nm from the acceptor, due to which their dipole moments resonate. Depending on the fluorescence of the acceptor, it can either absorb energy and fluoresce, or convert the resulting energy into heat [74].

Figure 5 shows the manufacturing methods of FRET-based biosensors, which are used for sensitively detecting various analytes using carbon dots. The fluorescent carbon dots shown in Fig. 5 (a) were obtained by applying the microwave heating method (720 W, 2 min) of a solution of water, citric acid, and ethylenediamine. The resulting brown product was dissolved in DI water, and then, as in the above-mentioned studies, was centrifuged and dialyzed (3500 Da, 3 days). In addition to carbon dots, this paper also presented the synthesis of gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) by reducing tetrachloraurate acid (HauCl4), the concentration of which was determined by the Lambert-Baer law.

The hydrothermal method for producing carbon dots from ortho-phenylenediamine (OPD) is described in Fig. 5 (b). As part of this method, OPD was dissolved in water and then placed in an autoclave for hydrothermal treatment (120 °C, 4 h). The resulting yellow-brown product, as previously described in Fig. 5 (a), was subjected to centrifugation, filtration, and dialysis (1000 Da, 24 h). The product was also subjected to vacuum freeze drying at the end of dialysis. The resulting carbon dots were further used for the detection of imidacloprid (IMI) by using the fluorescence energy transfer mechanism (FRET). When the analyte was added to a solution with fluorescent carbon dots, FL quenching was observed due to the FRET mechanism. As a result of the change in the concentration of IMI, the standard curve was constructed, based on which, as a result, it became possible to determine the concentration of analyte in samples based on the intensity of fluorescence.

Figure 5 (c) shows the sensor assembly process, the raw material for which is fresh apple seeds. In the framework of this study, apple seeds that had been pre-washed and dried were subjected to pyrolysis (300 °C, 1 h). As a result of thermal decomposition, a homogeneous black powder was obtained, which was subsequently mixed in cold water and centrifuged to remove large particles. Next, the supernatant was subjected to dialysis (12,000–14,000 Da, 24 h) to obtain an external solution from which a yellow-brown powder containing carbon dots was obtained by freeze-drying.

FRET has several advantages over traditional fluorescence, including its availability and relatively low cost [78]. Despite these advantages, several limitations of FRET need to be mentioned. One of the most significant disadvantages is the reduced signal-to-noise ratio (S/N) because the FRET signal occurs only in the presence of the target analyte [78]. Sensors with FRET are also sensible for changes in the environment, like pH, temperature, and concentrations [79]. The working principle of FRET is also limited by the distance of a few nanometers between labeled proteins, which is also found to be the disadvantage of this technique [80].

Traditional fluorescence biosensors usually known for their ability to easily quench fluorescence, which decreases sensitivity and stability of biosensors [81]. With the development of the carbon dots, researchers started their experiments based on the combinations of fluorescent biosensors and nanomaterials such as graphene or carbon dots [81]. Optical biosensors working principle of which is based on CDs are frequently used in different fields due to such advantages as fast response, high sensitivity, and relatively good photo-stability/low photo-bleaching [81]. Table 1 represents major analytical FoMs (Figures of Merit) from 31 analytical papers published after 2018+, which are dedicated to Carbon Dots-only biosensors. Carbon Dots-only sensors use non-conjugated (No use of antibodies or aptamers, etc) CDs.

Using reported limit of detection (LOD) values, the median LOD and geometric mean LOD were calculated. They were equal to 1.4*10^-7 M and 1.14*10^-7 M, respectively. The most sensitive biosensor was presented by Sun et al. in 2019. This research group presented the biosensor with fluorescent CDs that were used for alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) detection. This paper illustrates a biosensor that is not only sensitive (LOD was equal to 6.8*10^-12 M), but also reproducible, since the recovery and relative standard deviation of this sensor were equal to 97.05–102.00 % and 4.2 % respectively. Xu et al. also presented a biosensor used for trypsin detection that had an LOD significantly lower than the median one. According to this work, the LOD of the sensor with the detection of trypsin using FRET was equal to 3.5*10^-11 M. Table 1 illustrates good average value for relative standard deviation (RSD) which is equal to 3.8 %. We could expect low RSD value since Horwitz trumpet predicts this trend for methods with relatively high limit of detection of analytes.

Figure 6 represents the prominent analytical papers based on Carbon Dots-only biosensors. Fig. 6 (a) presents the fabrication process of smartphone-assisted paper-based biosensor used for rapid detection of carbendazim (CBZ). Guo et al. started the biosensor creation from deposition of Red-emissive cabon dots (R-CDs) on cellulose filter paper which was then tested by using smartphone. White papers at daylight were red when UV lamp was used. To evaluate the color of the paper strips under UV light, the images were made and color was detected by ‘Color Grab’ App built-in, which was previously downloaded to smartphone. Fig. 6 (b) presents the dependence of red-color intensity of the test paper excited by a 365 nm UV lamp on CBZ concentration. As it can be seen, the filter paper strips become darker with the increase in CBZ concentration from 0 to 2 μg/mL illustrating that fluorescent reduction of red emissive carbon dots happens when analyte concentration increases. Fig. 6 (c) demonstrates the synthetic route of Metal oxide hybrid with N-doped carbon dots (MFNCDs) preparation. In order to get MFNCDs, 2 solutions were mixed (solution 1: glucose and uric acid; solution 2: ethylene glycol (EG), MnCl2·4H2O, FeCl3·6H2O, and CH3COONa). After performing hydrothermal dialysis, orange under UV light and blue under fluorescent light MFNCDs were obtained. The schematic diagram of the injection spectrophotometric system (iSIS) system was presented in Fig. 6 (d). The system consisted of reservoir holder track, 7-port selection valve equipped with a 2.5 mL injection syringe, and detection system. Detection system contains spectrophotometer and universal flow cell. Flow cell and holder track were connected to the syringe by PTFE pipeline. Fig. 6 (e) illustrates the dependence of color intensity of solution on ATP concentration. As it can be mentioned, with increase in ATP concentration from 0 to 400 μM, the fluorescent intensity seen under UV light increases, whereas under normal light, the intensity of yellow color of the samples decreases. Nitrogen-enriched CDs synthesis using solvothermal method are presented in Fig. 6 (f). After addition of citric acid to urea under N, N-dimethylformamide, the mixture went through the solvothermal treatment. To achieve red emissive properties, CDs were treated with NaOH and HCl solution.

Enzymes are biological molecules that are used to facilitate biological reactions and the efficiency of their rate in human body [107]. This catalytic function makes the enzymatic biosensors more sensitive giving them ability to detect the analytes with lower limits of detection [108]. There are different types of enzymatic biosensors which use CDs for analytes detection due to specificity of enzymes, but in the same time enzymes which are bound with CDs improve the sensitivity and adaptability of the biosensors [107]. Table 2 analyzed 20 analytical papers about biosensors, the working principle of which is based on carbon dots-enzymatic detection.

The median and geometric mean of the limit of detection (LOD) presented in the papers were calculated and they were equal to 3.5*10^-7 M and 1.9*10^-7 M respectively. Mirzaei et al. presented the fluorometric biosensor for Cystatin C detection [124]. LOD of this sensor was equal to 3.8*10^-15 M and this parameter significantly differs from the calculated median value [124]. This research group used nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) that were functionalized with glutaraldehyde (NGQD-Glu). NGQD-Glu has lately been used to support the immobilization of papain (NGQD-Glu-Papain). The least sensitive paper presented in Table 2 was analytical work about fluorescent arginine detection with LOD equal to 9.0*10^-4 M presented by Sonthanasamy et al. [119] Despite relatively low sensitivity, authors presented the biosensor with high reproducibility (RSD is less than 1 %) and fast response time, which was equal to 10 min.

Analytical papers based on Carbon Dots-Enzymatic biosensors are presented in Fig. 7 . Fig. 7 (a) illustrates the fabrication process of cetyltriammonium bromide-nanocrystalline cellulose/quantum dots/tyrosinase enzyme/screen printed carbon electrode (CTAB-NCC/QDs/Tyr/SPCE) biosensor. Tyrosinase enzyme (blue copper protein) catalyzes the phenol oxidation process to o-quinones in the presence of oxygen which is then reduced to water. After this step, o-quinone was reduced to catechol at the surface of the electrode, producing electrocatalytic current response. This makes this biosensor sensitive for phenol detection. Molecular docking of Diazinon with Honey bee acetylcholinesterase (AChE) is illustrated in Fig. 7 (b). This interaction happens at allosteric site of enzyme, were Trp116 and Tyr385 make π-π interactions, whereas Gly167 and Tyr389 make strong hydrogen bonds with inhibitor. Fig. 7 (c) presents the working mechanism of glucose probing system based on S-CDs. Glucose oxidase (GOx) catalyzes the glucose to hydrogen peroxide, which stimulates the aggresome production by the oxidation of S-CDs. Preparation of one-pot CDs is shown in Fig. 7 (d). This carbon dots were synthesized by hydrothermal method (12 h at 170 °C) and accomplished by combining folic acid (FA) and p-phenylenediamine (PPD) as precursors. Fig. 7 (e) demonstrates the photographic images of diacrylated poly(ethylene glycol)/l-ascorbic acid/acryl groups-functionalized CD/rhodamine 6G/glucose oxidase/horseradish peroxidase (DAPEG/AA/A-CD/Rh6G/GOx/HRP) film. As it can be seen, in the presence of UV light, colorless film becomes blue, demonstrating the fluorescent properties of CDs that were used in its fabrication.

The use of CDs have gained attention in antigen-antibody immunosensors due to their multiple advantages such as low toxicity, high biocompatibility, highly sensitive detection and high electrochemical response [127]. CDs were prevalent in analytical techniques as fluorescence, various electrochemical methods, electrochemiluminescence, etc. It was noted that most of non-catalytic protein biomarkers are based on electrochemiluminescence, photoelectrochemistry, and photoluminescence methods [128]. Conductive characteristics of immune complexes that form due to CDs allow for working mechanism of the biosensor based on electrochemiluminescence [128].

33 articles were analyzed for their analytical performance of detecting based on antigen – antibody interactions. Table 3 summarises information for their limit of detection, linear or dynamic range and RSD values including their average and range.

Figure 8 (a) represents the integration of the fluorescence (FL) and surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) techniques to a probe for detection norovirus like particles using sulfur-doped agar-derived carbon dots (S-agCDs) [138]. Adachu et al. suggested biosensor that achieved 0.95 fg/mL limit of detection in 10 % human serum as demonstrated in Fig. 8 (a) [138]. Since SERS technique allowed the sensitive detection of the norovirus antigen, it was suggested that further quantification can be applied for clinical applications [138]. Similarly, Shao et al. demonstrated the use of the fluorescence aptasensor for detection of carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) technique [132]. The aptasensor consisted of near-infrared carbon dots (NIR-CDs) and gold nanorods (AuNRs), TEM images of which are represented in Fig. 8 (b) [132]. There are visible small dots around the shells that are considered to be NIR-CDs of the aptasensor depicted in Fig. 8 (b) [132]. The aptasensor showed high sensitivity with a limit of detection that is 0.02 pg/mL [132]. Wang et al. represented similar technique for the detection of CEA which includes fluorescence-infrared absorption [139]. The combined use of CDs and SiO2 improved the sensitivity of detection by enhancing the fluorescence signal [139]. Fig. 8 (c) shows the CDs height and size profiles by AFM. Their suggested method had limit of detection 0.0008 pg/mL comparatively lower than using techniques such as photoelectrochemistry, electrochemical, and with carbon nanotube [139]. Although there is an electrochemical method based on electrochemiluminescence resonance energy transfer with synthesis of CdTe coated CDs that achieved satisfying sensitive detection with LOD 6.3 fg/mL [152]. According to schematic construction of the sensor to detect the squamous cell carcinoma in Fig. 8 (d), CDs transfer energy to CdTe by improving the electrochemiluminescence signal and overall performance of the sensor [152]. Another electrochemical method with high sensitivity was suggested by Krathumkhet et al. to detect CA125 [148]. The analytical performance of the sensor was enhanced by antibody interactions on ITO substrate with a help of CDs and the sensor had limit of detection as 0.1 fg/mL [148]. As shown on Fig. 8 (e) the CDs are synthesized using citric acid and ethylenediamine and carbonization was done using hydrothermal process [148]. There is also an electrochemical method for detection of PSA with a combination of CQDs and gold nanoparticles with LOD of 2 fg/mL [146]. Despite TEM images, there was FTIR spectra for characterization of CQDs synthesis with distinct carbonyl group stretching vibrations at 1700 cm−1 shown in Fig. 8 (f) [146]. According to spectra, the intensity of the carbonyl group bands were lowered by the addition of the gold nanoparticles to the surface of CQDs [146].

The performance of the biosensors was evaluated for common electrochemical, electrochemiluminescence, fluorescence, and colorimetric methods by calculating the median and geometric mean LOD values. The results are provided in Table 4 .

Xu et al. underlined the advantages of carbon quantum dots such as low toxicity at cellular lever and presence of carboxyl and hydroxyl functional groups that led to widespread use of CQDs in biosensing and nanomedicine [155]. Their group suggested to use perylenetetracarboxylic acid (PTCA) as it produces strong ECL signal in a reaction with S2O4 2− in a range of −0.2 V to −1.8 V [155]. The detection principle designed in this paper allowed to reach the lowest limit of detection in Table 3 as of 1.44E-21 based on the synergistic ECL effect by PTCA, CQDs, and S2O4 2− [155]. High sensitivity of detection permitted the application of the sensor in human serum samples for various types of cancer such liver, breast, colon, and stomach. Since the results of tests demonstrated satisfactory range of relative standard error from 2.43 % to 4.03 %, the immunosensor has potential for the clinical diagnosis for the CEA biomarker [155]. Similarly, Pan's group developed a fluorescence aptasensor using NIR-CDs and gold nanorods for sensitive detection of the CEA with LOD of 1.11E-16 [132]. The sensor was applied for human pleural effusion samples and resulted satisfactory recovery values and low RSD in a range from 1.3 % to 3.7 % [132]. Thus, Shao et al. developed an aptasensor with a potential to be applied for human samples for the detection of CEA [132].

Another promising sensor was proposed using the similar electrochemical technique designed for the detection of ovarian cancer. Krathumkhet et al. fabricated an immunosensor based on the sandwich structure carbon ink/carbon dot/zine oxide electrodes that detects CA-125 antigen with silver nanoparticles improving the chemical stability [148]. The limit of detection of this biosensor reached 5.00E-19. Real test analysis was conducted in human blood serum samples showing the potential of the immunosensor for further use in the clinical diagnosis of the CA-125 biomarker in human serum [148].

The use of the of CD-based antigen-antibody in clinical diagnostic were also suggested by Achadu et al. for the detection of norovirus. Their group prepared the biosensors using magnetic nanoparticles to enhance the sensitivity of the immunoassay and remove the matric interferences in a combination with silver nanoparticles and polydopamine [138]. Following this approach, their sensor reached the limit of detection as 1.67E-18. The sensitivity of this biosensor detected 10 copies mL−1 of NoV infected patients, which suggested it is a promising tool for the further use in clinical samples [138].

The carbon nanomaterials based antigen-antibody biosensors have been applied for the detection of antibodies produced from the infective diseases and biomarkers associated with those virus infections [159]. Specifically for CD-based biosensors, they demonstrated their effectiveness for Japanese encephalitis, Zika, dengue, and herpes simplex virus type 1 (HSV-1) [160]. As an example, CQD derived from a benzoxazine monomer identified to be able to block infections such as Zika, dengue viruses, and Japanese encephalitis [160]. The integration of CDs for the biosensing of cancer biomarkers is also promising field due to rapid and sensitive detection [161]. The biosensors based on antigen-antibody interactions commonly detected CA125, CEA, and PSA [135,146,148,154,155]. As an example for CEA detection with high sensitivity, Wang et al. demonstrated the FL-IRA dual-mode biosensor with excellent performance in recovery in serum detection and suggested this method to be integrated for on-site detection [139]. Another promising design for the detection of PSA was suggested by Pourmadadi et al. that was an electrochemical aptasensor modified with Au/carbon quantum dots via AuS bonding [146]. The response was also tested on real samples and it illustrated its effectiveness for them [146].

Carbon dots, which are <10 nm size quasi-spherical nanoparticles consisting of carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen atoms, are famous for their good luminophore properties [162]. However, the ELC emission of bare carbon dots is low and not enough for sensitive detection of low concentration biomarkers, hence such methods as heteroatom doping was used to increase the ECL intensity of CDs [47]. Due to restricted active sites, multiatom co-doping in CDs is preferable than single atom doping. Usually, N/S, N/P and N/B co-doping is used, however there are also examples of Se/N and F/N co/doping [45,47]. In detection of nucleic acids, CDs and CDs based nanocomposites finds their application in detection of HIV, HBV, HTLV-1 and SARS-CoV-2 DNA fragments as well as microRNAs.

MicroRNAs, which are small endogenous non-coding RNA fragments 19–24 nucleotides long, are found to play crucial role in silencing target genes posttranscriptionally [163,164]. Even a single miRNA molecule can influence transcription of numerous genes by targeting hundreds of messenger RNA molecules [164]. Since miRNAs regulate the expression of many tumor suppressor and oncogenes, they are considered to predict tumorigenesis [163,165]. Small size, low expression, high homology and susceptibility to degradation put a serious challenge for the detection of miRNA, that is why development of highly sensitive sensors is required [166]. Apart from that, according to Qui & Hildebrandt, ideal point of care miRNA assays should be single-step to minimize washing and preparation steps, multiplexed to simultaneously measure several different miRNAs, without complicated preparation procedures and sophisticated equipment, reproducible and robust, and stable within kit assay format [167]. Electrochemiluminescence, along with fluorescence is one of common miRNA detection methods, since it provides high sensitivity, perfect selectivity, high signal to noise ratio and fast response [168,169]. Even though the application of bare carbon dots in ECL assays is limited because of their weak ECL emissions and wide excitation potentials, they can undergo heteroatom doping or be used with co-reactants such as DNA, hydrogen peroxide, peroxodisulfate, etc. [40,170,171] Table 5 shows the compilation of CD-based nucleic acids biosensors.

One of the common biomarkers which is detected with carbon dots is microRNA-21. It was one of the first detected oncomicroRNAs which targets tumor suppressor genes, thus being a cancer promoter [196]. The geometric mean of the LOD values for microRNA-21 detection from 12 analyzed studies was 1.01E-15, which shows the importance of highly sensitive sensors due to low concentration of microRNA-21. The most sensitive values with aM to tens of aM range were achieved with electrochemiluminescence- and fluorescence-based detection. Lowest LOD among analyzed studies for the detection of miRNA-21 with carbon dots was 2.50 aM. Li et al. (2022) developed a silica ring-based ratiometric fluorescence nanosensing platform with a background correction system. After silica nanorods with variable length are bent in circular structure centered on the CDs, AuNPs were attached to the ring-shaped silica structures, and nucleic acids were conjugated on top of them. Wheel-like CDs-SiO2-AuNPs-DNA probe (CSAD) was now formed with AuNPs acting as fluorescence quenchers of fluorophores. While the originally quenched 6-carboxyfluorescein outside the ring showed re-emitted fluorescence emission signal, fluorescence intensity of the carbon dots inside the ring has not changed, which was used to decrease the background noise. [182]This method with its 7 orders of magnitude wide logarithmic range have demonstrated better performance compared with optical detection method reported previously.

Mohammadi et al. (2021) in their study achieved high sensitivity of 0.03 fM. CDs were synthesized from glutaraldehyde and ethanol and were heated in the oven. Prevention of CDs aggregation using CDs-CH nanocomposite hydrogels increased their photostability. Incomplete quenching of CDs-CH nanocomposite hydrogels was achieved by the specific interaction between DNA probe and target microRNA-21, hence higher concentrations of microRNA-21 resulted in lower fluorescence intensity. Selectivity of this biosensor was tested with microRNA-155, one base mismatched microRNA-155 and one base mismatched microRNA-21, none of which have interfered with the microRNA-21 detection. Authors also report that CDs-CH nanocomposite hydrogels showed higher selectivity than GA-CDs and NB-CDs [55].

For DNA, the most sensitive biosensor was developed by Zibadi et al. for the detection of HTLV-1 DNA fragments with outstanding performance. 0.7 aM limit of detection in their study was achieved by differential pulse voltammetry. CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method from Caccinia Marcanthera. Utilization of CQDs@Au/GCE allowed for highly sensitive detection without nucleic acid amplification. Biosensor was tested on 40 real clinical samples of peripheral blood mononuclear cell, 10 of which were negative and 30 of which were positive. For the negative samples electrochemical signal was close to zero since no hybridization reaction have occured, while positive samples have shown significant electrochemical intensity. Sensor have exhibited 100 % sensitivity and 97 % specificity, as well as showed high selectivity for HTLV-1 when tested with HBV and HIV contaminated real clinical samples [195].

Figure 9a shows the process of preparation of CQDs and CQDs@Au. For the synthesis of CQDs Zibadi et al. used Caccinia Macranthera plant powder, 30 g of which was dissolved in double-distilled water and stirred for 2 h. The obtained solution was then filtered through filter paper, transferred to stainless-steel autoclave with Teflon lining, and put in an oven for 4 h at 180 deg. C. After the autoclave was cooled down, the obtained mixture was placed in the centrifuge for 15 min at 4000 rpm for settling any unreacted substances. After centrifugation the solution was again filtered using the filter paper to exclude any unreacted species. To synthesize CQDs@Au, Zibadi et al. added 5 mL of CQDs mixture to the solution of 3 mL 0.01 M HAuCl4 and 1 mL of 0.05 M NH3. After transferring the obtained mixture to 50 mL beaker, it was dissolved with double-distilled water, and stirred at room temperature for 30 min. The prepared CQDs@Au nanocomposite was stored at 4 deg. C for two months. This nanocomposite was used as a new core-shell material to modify the surface of GCEs (Fig. 9b), which were used for differential pulse voltammetry measurements and allowed for outstanding sensitivity with detection limit of 0.7 aM, and 10 orders of magnitude wide linear range from 10 aM to 100 nM [195].

Figure 9c demonstrates the scheme of Y-shape-structured biosensor developed by Zhong et al. for the detection of miRNA-222. Poly(2-amino-terephthalic acid) (ATA) polymer film was electropolymerized and activated on glassy carbon electrode. Loading of AuNPs on this surface allowed for decreasing impedance and increasing ECL intensity. Dual AuN bonds allowed for immobilization of hairpin capture DNA probe modified with NH2 on the AuNPs/ATA/GCE surface. Modification of both capture and signal probe with locked nucleic acid (LNA) helped to reduce the conformational flexibility of ribose and improved the selectivity for complementary sequences. ECL signals were obtained from CQDs-PEI-GO labeled with signal DNA probe after miRNA binding and formation of Y-shaped junction structures. In the absence of miRNA little or no ECL signal was produced. This biosensor had demonstrated high selectivity towards miRNA-222 comparing to miRNA-21, miRNA-126, let7a, fully-mismatched-RNA and blank, which is visualized on the graph at Fig. 9d. It has also allowed for excellent sensitivity and the capacity to discriminate a single-base mismatch. This biosensor had demonstrated the limit of detection as low as 1.95 fM and 3.301 orders of magnitude wide linear range [163].

Yuan/Chai's group created an electrochemiluminescence biosensor using selenium‑nitrogen co-doped carbon dots which has shown highly efficient ELC and outstanding biocompatibility. Fig. 9e demonstrates the stability of this biosensor for 20 cycles of continuous scanning. RSD value of twenty measurements was considerably low – 1.81 %. The sensor has also demonstrated excellent sensitivity with LOD being 2.3 aM, and wide linear range of 7 orders of magnitude from 10 aM to 100 pM [47]. Fig. 9f from another study by Yuan/Chai's group demonstrates the calibration curve for the detection of HIV-DNA fragments in the range of 10 aM to 10 pM which in total gives 6 orders of magnitude. In their work fluorine‑nitrogen co-doped carbon dots were used as a stable and strong electrochemiluminescence emitter in the biosensor for human immunodeficiency virus DNA fragments detection. Apart from the wide linear range, they had shown attomolar sensitivity with 6.34 aM limit of detection [45]. Divya et al. had developed sensitive and selective platform based on carbon dots stabilized silver nanoparticles lipid nano hybrids, which allow for label-free electrochemical detection of DNA which was not previously reported. Fig. 9g shows the HRTEM image of lipid-CD-AgNPs. Black dots represent the distribution of carbon dots in and around the lipid. Use of MPA-lipid-CD-AgNPs on gold electrode allowed for sensitive detection of cDNA with 0.1 fM detection limit and 5 order of magnitude wide linear range [191].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of detection methods for CD-based nucleic acids biosensors
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate performance of different biosensors we have grouped them by the method of detection and calculated the geometric mean of LOD, median of LOD, mean of logarithmic range and mean of average RSD. The results can be seen in Table 6 .

As shown in Table 6, the most sensitive method of detection for CD-based nucleic acids biosensors was electrochemiluminescence with geometric mean of LOD of it being lower than that of fluorescence and electrochemical methods – 8.60*10−17 M vs. 6.03*10−12 M and 5.79*10−16 M respectively. However, medians of LOD of electrochemiluminescence and electrochemical biosensors were almost the same and showed a serious different from the fluorescence-based biosensors – 3.00*10−17 M and 9.80*10−17 M vs. 8.65*10−12 M. Based on 11 studies, electrochemiluminescence biosensors have also demonstrated wide logarithmic range – 5.594, and low RSD – 2.432 %, which makes ELC a promising technique for the detection of nucleic acids by CD-based biosensors. Among analyzed studies, most fluorescence-based biosensors were developed in 2017–2021, while ECL was mostly used in more recent biosensors from 2023 to 2024. Compared to fluorescence, the energy from excitation source in ECL assays has different nature than the signal, which open the door for such advantages of ECL as high signal to noise ratio [171].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of analytical performance of the CD-based biosensors
ce:section.ce:para: We have analyzed the analytical performance of four types of CD-based biosensors by comparing the geometric mean and median of LOD for each type. The medan and geometric mean LOD are calculated based on published LOD, which were tabulated in Tables 1, 2, 3, 5 above and those results are shown in Table 7 .

As shown in Table 7, CD-based antigen-antibody and nucleic acids biosensors are the most sensitive among four types of biosensors with geometric mean of LOD being for the latter almost being lower – 1.09*10−13 M and 5.54*10−14 M respectively, which indicates better analytical performance of nucleic acids biosensors. Median values also support this statement with nucleic acids biosensors showing slightly lower result – 4.00*10−14 vs. 8.34*10−14 for antigen-antibody biosensors. High performance of nucleic acids biosensors might relate to the fact that they present only in low concentration, so excellent sensitivity is required for these sensors to be applicable [166]. Antigen-antibody biosensors have shown the lowest minimal value of LOD – 1.44*10−21. However, even with wide linear range of 1 ag mL−1 to 1 ng/mL this biosensor for the detection of carcinoembryonic antigen does not capture the whole normal range of this protein's concentration – from 0 to 2.5 ng/mL [197]. Least sensitivity was exhibited by enzymatic biosensors – geometric mean of LOD was only 1.91*10−7 M and median was 3.51*10−7 M.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Correlation between LOD and Size/Diameter of the CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The relationship between the limit of detection (LOD) and the diameter of carbon dots (CDs) is an important factor in determining their sensing capabilities. A smaller LOD typically indicates higher sensitivity, making it crucial to assess whether CD size plays a significant role in detection performance. To investigate this, we analyzed data from 17 publications where both LOD and CD measured diameters represented on Table 8 .

As illustrated in Fig. 10 , analysis the data from publications presented in Table 8, we found no statistically significant correlation between these two parameters, with the coefficient of determination (R2) for the trendline being extremely low (0.0008). Similarly, Fig. 11 presents the correlation between -log(LOD) and CD diameter, yielding an R2 value of 0.0037. These findings suggest that CD diameter alone is unlikely to have a significant influence on sensitivity/ detection limits in biosensing based on CD fluorescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Correlation between LOD and year of publication
ce:section.ce:para: We also have analyzed the dependence of detection limit on the year of publication for each of the four types of CD-based biosensors.

Figure 12 illustrates the correlation between -log(LOD) and year of publication for four types of CD-based biosensors. R2 linear regression coefficient values demonstrate practically NO correlation (R2 < 0.01) between -log(LOD) and year of publication for each type of CD-based biosensors except the ones for nucleic acids targeting biosensors. For the latter R2 was 0.3803 which indicates some positive correlation. It demonstrates saturation of sensitivity improvement in most types of CD based biosensors, except CD-based sensors for nucleic acids, which still demonstrate some sensitivity improvement in the last several years (from 2018). It may be interesting that the lowest LOD value among all considered analytical papers, 1.44*10−21 M for the detection of carcinoembryonic antigen, was reported by Xu et al. in 2018 [155]. Still we believe that this kind of saturation in improvement of CD-based sensing can be overcome by rapid development of AI and machine learning, which may assist in improvement of sensitivity as well as reproducibility and reliability of CD-based biosensors.
Machine Learning (ML) is the collection of data and algorithms to analyze and process this data, which is used to make some predictions during and after analysis [211].

The combination of biosensors and ML is frequently used to reduce noise, detect anomalies, and automate the detection process [212]. Considering this advantages, it can be outlined that machine learning method and artificial intelligence techniques are used to overcome the challenges that usually disrupt the working process of biosensors [212]. Nowadays, there are types of biosensors known as “Intelligent biosensors”, some of them has a feature to detect the concentration of analytes in the sample using AI and ML techniques [212].

Several of ML methods started gaining attraction in 2019–2020 for application in sensors to address data-related problems and enable automatic prediction of analyte concentrations through ML-based algorithms: neural networks were applied to physical sensors like acceleration and strain sensors [218]. As for ML in healthcare, a prediction of the three-dimensional structure of proteins using only the sequence of amino acids were offered by AI system Alphafold2, winning Critical Assessment of Structure Prediction (CASP) in 1994 [219].

He et al. conducted the study aiming to develop an effective ML model, forecasting inhibition efficiency and optimize the synthesis of CD-based corrosion inhibitors, addressing the limitations of trial-and-error methods, such as randomness, subjectivity and contingency [213]. As shown in Fig. 13 (a), the data was randomly grouped into a 9:1 ratio for validation and root-mean-square error (RMSE), mean absolute error (MAE), and R2 metrics are applied to evaluate different ML models [213]. Random Forest (RF) is identified as the most effective based on its lowest RMSE and MAE values, and comparatively high R2 scores, as illustrated in Fig. 13 (a). This underlined this method's faster speed, accuracy, robustness, and resistance to overfitting [213]. Wang et al. investigated different ML models to determine third-order nonlinear coefficient and computed boxplot tests, showing that Gradient-Boosted Decision Trees (GBDT) model outperformed other models in predicting the χ(3) [214]. According to Fig. 13 (b), GBDT achieved high predictive accuracy with Rtrain of 91 % and Rtest of 97 % [214]. To improve the output accuracy of ML models, Zhang et al. combine Kmeans and Red-Blue-Green feature extraction, aiming to determine the suitable K parameter [215]. Fig. 13 (c) demonstrates eight center points' distribution for blank and its 60 μg L−1 Cr(VI) and shows that as concentration increased, high-brightness clusters diminished while low-brightness clusters became more visible, emphasizing the benefit of RGB and cluster features [215]. Thongsai et al. showed that computational methods elucidate the sensing abilities of CDs [216]. The provided spectra in Fig. 13 (d) aligned with experimental data for the UV–vis absorption, confirming the accuracy of our computational models [216]. The development of ML, specifically through deep learning that use deep convolutional neural networks (DCNN), has enabled models that can predict the optical properties such as spectral characteristics and fluorescence color for CDs, highlighting DCNN's potential in assisting the CD synthesis [217].

Biosensors in which the detection principle is based on fabrication and usage of carbon dots illustrate high sensitivity and reproducibility. By analyzing biofluids in which real sample analysis was performed, it can be concluded, that human blood serum is most frequently used. A possible reason for the usage of human serum is the possible applications of fabricated sensors in real clinics or hospitals for biosensing and biomarker detection.

Another example, the work presented by Wu's research group illustrates the process of fabrication of biosensors for rapid detection of multi-antibiotics like sulfamethazine (SMZ), oxytetracycline (OTC), and chloramphenicol (CAP) [93]. Working principle of this biosensor includes the usage of a smartphone for color recognition and 3-D printed device for rapid detection of multiple antibiotics. Such parts of biosensors make it cost-effective, portable, sensitive, and reproducible. Therefore, this method is promising since it can be improved by designing of micro-optical parts and reduction in the weight and size of the device, as well as enhancement of its performance.

We have been searching, but have NOT found any reports about commercial applications in sensing for CDs so far, although Akin et al. have developed the biosensor for microRNA-141 with smartphone readout [193]. However, there are some reports presenting commercial use of quantum dots already. DTechEx reports that there are already commercial QD based product on a market (e.g low-cost hybrid QD-based image sensors, etc) and their forecast that global QD materials market predicts doubling of QD global output by weight between 2024 and 2030 [220]. We would expect that the same trends would be followed by global output of CDs. Some promising sensing methods developed for QD can be potentially transferred for the application with carbon dots, including for instance applications of cell phones as reader instead of bench top readers. The example of smartphone connected readout in point-of-care quantum dots-based devices is reported in several publications [221–223]. Cell phone detection is likely to make substantial decrease in instrumental and operational cost of analysis in comparison with benchtop based methods. We believe that CD applications would go along the same path and there is strong potential for the possible commercialization of carbon dot in sensing applications in the foreseeable future. Development of Machine Learning Methods for sensing may also nudge compact self-phone operated CD-based biosensors for a wide commercial, clinical, and even household application.
